fact: Lucien Laurent of France scored the first goal in FIFA World Cup history — vs Mexico, 13 July 1930, in the 19th minute.

Bert Patenaude (USA) scored the first hat-trick in World Cup history vs Paraguay on 17 July 1930. FIFA only officially recognised it in 2006 — for decades the credit went to Argentina's Guillermo Stábile.

Plácido Galindo of Peru was the first player ever sent off at a World Cup — vs Romania, 14 July 1930, in the 56th minute. (Red and yellow cards as physical objects didn't appear until 1970; in 1930 dismissals were verbal.)
1930 had no third-place playoff. FIFA later ranked the United States 3rd and Yugoslavia 4th retroactively, splitting them on goal difference (USA +1, Yugoslavia 0).
1930 had 13 teams — Group 1 had four (Argentina, Chile, France, Mexico), the other three groups had three each. Group winners advanced directly to the semi-finals.
By winning the inaugural World Cup, Uruguay completed a treble: 1924 Olympic gold, 1928 Olympic gold, and 1930 World Cup gold — all within six years.

Only four European nations (France, Belgium, Romania, Yugoslavia) made the long sea voyage to Uruguay for the inaugural World Cup. Several major European football powers — England, Italy, Germany, Spain — declined to send teams.
Argentina's Guillermo Stábile won the inaugural Golden Boot with 8 goals despite Argentina losing the final 4-2 to Uruguay.

1934 is the only World Cup ever to use a pure single-elimination format — every match from the round of 16 onward was do-or-die. With 16 teams entering and 17 matches played, no team could lose and stay alive.

Reigning champions Uruguay refused to defend their title in 1934, retaliating against the European nations that had skipped 1930. They are still the only World Cup champion ever to skip the next edition.
1934 was the first World Cup to require qualification — 32 nations competed for 16 spots. Even host Italy had to play a qualifier (vs Greece).

Luis Monti is the only player ever to play in two World Cup finals for two different countries — for Argentina in the 1930 final (lost 2-4 to Uruguay) and for Italy in the 1934 final (won 2-1 vs Czechoslovakia).

Italy's 1934 squad included multiple oriundi — South-American-born players naturalised as Italian — Luis Monti, Raimundo Orsi, Enrique Guaita, Atilio Demaría, Anfilogino Guarisi, and Mario Varglien (all Argentine or Brazilian-born).
Egypt was the first African — and first non-European, non-American — nation to play at a World Cup. They lost 4-2 to Hungary in the round of 16, with Abdulrahman Fawzi scoring both Egyptian goals.

Italy and Spain drew 1-1 after extra time in the 1934 quarter-final, then replayed the next day (1 June) — Italy won 1-0. Seven Spanish starters from match one were too injured to play the replay, including legendary goalkeeper Ricardo Zamora.

The 1934 final went to extra time. Czechoslovakia led 1-0 through Antonín Puč in the 71st minute; Raimundo Orsi equalised in the 81st; Angelo Schiavio scored the winner in the 95th — the first World Cup final ever decided in extra time.

Austria qualified for 1938 but withdrew after the March 1938 Anschluss annexed it into Nazi Germany. Sweden received a walkover into the quarter-finals. Several Austrian players (Sindelar, Bican) refused to join the German national team afterwards — Sindelar died mysteriously a few months later. Eight ex-Austrian players were forced into Germany's 1938 squad post-Anschluss.
Vittorio Pozzo of Italy is the only manager ever to win two FIFA World Cups (1934 + 1938). Italy in 1938 became the first nation to retain the World Cup title.

Brazil 6-5 Poland in the 1938 round of 16 was an extra-time goal-fest with hat-tricks on both sides — Leônidas for Brazil and Ernst Wilimowski for Poland. Wilimowski's 4 goals in a single match remain a World Cup record matched only a handful of times since.
Cuba made their only-ever World Cup appearance in 1938 and reached the quarter-finals (after upsetting Romania in a R16 replay). They have never qualified again.

1938 needed three replays — Switzerland-Germany (R16), Cuba-Romania (R16), and Brazil-Czechoslovakia (QF) — bringing the total to 18 matches in 16 days. Penalty shootouts didn't yet exist; ties went to a full replay 24-48 hours later.

1950 is the only World Cup with no formal final. After group stage, four teams (Uruguay, Brazil, Sweden, Spain) played a round-robin final pool, with title decided by points. The last match — Uruguay 2-1 Brazil at the Maracanã — happened to be the de-facto title decider only because of how the standings worked out.

The Maracanazo: Uruguay 2-1 Brazil at the Maracanã, 16 July 1950. Hosts Brazil needed only a draw, led 1-0 through Friaça (47'), but Schiaffino (66') and Ghiggia (79') scored for Uruguay. Attendance was 199,854 — still the largest crowd ever at a football match.

Uruguay won their second World Cup in 1950 with arguably the smallest squad path to glory ever — they only played one group match (an 8-0 win over Bolivia, after France withdrew from their 2-team group), then three final-pool matches.

USA 1-0 England, 29 June 1950 in Belo Horizonte — Joe Gaetjens scored the only goal. England, on their World Cup debut, were heavy favourites; the result is one of the great upsets in football history. Some British newspapers reportedly received the score as 10-1 to England and printed it that way.
1950 was the first World Cup in 12 years — World War II forced cancellation of the 1942 (planned for Brazil or Germany) and 1946 editions. The 12-year gap remains the longest in World Cup history.

India qualified for 1950 but withdrew before the tournament. A popular myth says they refused because FIFA banned barefoot play; the actual reasons were travel costs and Indian football authorities prioritising the Olympics. India have never since qualified.

England, Yugoslavia (returning post-war), and the United States (returning) appeared at their first World Cup with the modern home-nations rules. England were eliminated in the group stage on their debut.

The 'Miracle of Bern' — Hungary's Magic Magyars (unbeaten in 32 internationals over four years and reigning Olympic champions) led West Germany 2-0 after 8 minutes of the final, but Germany came back to win 3-2. Helmut Rahn scored the winner. It is widely considered the greatest upset in football history.
1954 still holds the record for goals per match — 5.38 (140 goals in 26 matches). The QF Austria 7-5 Switzerland is the highest-scoring single match in World Cup history.
1954 was the first World Cup where players wore numbered jerseys — required by FIFA after the 1950 edition.
The 'Battle of Bern' (Hungary 4-2 Brazil QF, 27 June 1954) saw three players sent off in a brutally violent encounter, with the brawling continuing in the dressing rooms after the final whistle.
Hungary's Sándor Kocsis won the Golden Boot with 11 goals, the second-highest single-tournament total ever (behind Just Fontaine's 13 in 1958).

France's Just Fontaine scored 13 goals at the 1958 World Cup — still the single-tournament record. Four came in the third-place match alone (France 6-3 West Germany). Fontaine never played another World Cup; a leg break in 1960 effectively ended his career.
Pelé made his World Cup debut at 17, scoring 6 goals including a hat-trick in the SF vs France and two in the final. He remains the youngest player ever to score in a World Cup final.

1958 is the only World Cup to have all four British home nations qualify — England, Scotland, Wales, and Northern Ireland. Wales and Northern Ireland reached the QFs; Scotland went out in the group stage.
1958 is one of the few World Cups where no own goals were credited — all 126 goals were credited to the scoring team's players.

Six players tied for top scorer at 1962 with 4 goals each — Garrincha, Vavá (Brazil), Leonel Sánchez (Chile), Flórián Albert (Hungary), Valentin Ivanov (USSR), Dražan Jerković (Yugoslavia). FIFA officially awarded the Golden Boot to Garrincha. The most-tied Golden Boot in WC history.

The 'Battle of Santiago' (Chile 2-0 Italy, 2 June 1962) is often cited as the most violent match in World Cup history. Two Italians were sent off; English referee Ken Aston later invented the yellow/red card system after officiating a chaotic 1966 incident, partly inspired by 1962.

Pelé suffered a tournament-ending injury in Brazil's second group game vs Czechoslovakia. Garrincha and Amarildo (his replacement) carried Brazil to a back-to-back title — the second time a nation defended the World Cup (after Italy 1934-1938).

Geoff Hurst's hat-trick in the 1966 final (England 4-2 West Germany, after extra time) is the only hat-trick ever scored in a World Cup final. The third goal — a shot off the underside of the crossbar — remains controversial: did it cross the line? Russian linesman Tofiq Bahramov said yes.
World Cup Willie — a lion in a Union Jack jersey — was the first World Cup mascot. Every World Cup since 1966 has had one.
Eusébio (Portugal) won the Golden Boot with 9 goals, including 4 in the QF as Portugal came back from 3-0 down vs North Korea to win 5-3 — still one of the great World Cup comebacks.

North Korea, on their World Cup debut, beat reigning Group 4 favourites Italy 1-0 (Pak Doo-ik, 42'). It is one of the most famous upsets in football history; the Italian squad were pelted with rotten tomatoes on their return home.

The Jules Rimet Trophy was stolen from a public exhibition in London in March 1966, four months before the tournament. It was found a week later under a hedge in South London by a dog named Pickles. The trophy was stolen again — and never recovered — in Brazil in 1983.

The 'Game of the Century' — Italy 4-3 West Germany SF, 17 June 1970 at the Estadio Azteca. Tied 1-1 after 90 minutes; FIVE goals in extra time. Italy went up 2-1; Schnellinger equalised 2-2 in the 90th; Müller 3-2; Burgnich 3-3; Riva 4-3; Müller 4-4 in 110th... no wait, Riva 4-3 then Rivera 4-3, then Italy held on after Müller 3-3. The match is commemorated by a plaque outside the stadium.

By winning a third title in 1970, Brazil were granted permanent ownership of the Jules Rimet Trophy under FIFA's three-wins rule. The trophy was stolen from the Brazilian football federation in 1983 and never recovered — believed to have been melted down for the gold.

Mário Zagallo became the first man to win the World Cup as both player (1958, 1962) and coach (1970). Only Franz Beckenbauer (1974 player, 1990 coach) and Didier Deschamps (1998 player, 2018 coach) have matched the feat since.

By winning the 1970 final, Pelé became the only player ever to win three World Cup winner's medals (1958, 1962, 1970). Two other players have won three (Cafu, Pirlo had 2 finals appearances) but only Pelé won three winners.
1970 was the first WC to use yellow and red cards (after Ken Aston's invention) and the first to allow substitutes (2 per team). It was also the first WC broadcast in colour worldwide.

Rinus Michels' Netherlands debuted 'Total Football' (Totaalvoetbal) at 1974 — every outfield player able to play any role. Led by Johan Cruyff, the team revolutionised the sport. They reached the final undefeated but lost 2-1 to West Germany.

East Germany 1-0 West Germany on 22 June 1974 (Hamburg) was the only meeting of the two German states at a World Cup. Jürgen Sparwasser's goal stunned the hosts. Both teams advanced; East Germany was eliminated in round 2.
1974 introduced the new FIFA World Cup Trophy (designed by Silvio Gazzaniga, made of solid 18-carat gold) after Brazil kept the Jules Rimet permanently in 1970. It is the trophy still in use today.

The 1974 and 1978 World Cups used a unique two-group-stage format (no traditional semi-finals): 4 groups of 4 → top 2 advance to one of two second-round groups → group winners contested the final, runners-up the third-place playoff. The format returned in 1982 in modified form.

Johan Cruyff did not play in the 1978 World Cup — long believed to be a protest against Argentina's military junta, but Cruyff later revealed (in 2008) the real reason was a kidnap attempt on his family. Without him, the Netherlands still reached the final.

Argentina needed to beat Peru by exactly 4+ goals in their last 2nd-round match to advance over Brazil on goal difference. They won 6-0. Allegations of a fixed match — including grain shipments and political prisoners — have persisted for decades but never been proven.

Mario Kempes won both the Golden Boot (6 goals) and the Golden Ball at 1978 — only the third player to do that double after Pelé (1970) and Garrincha (1962). His two goals in the final secured Argentina's first title.

1982 was the first World Cup with 24 teams (expanded from 16). Format: 6 groups of 4 → 4 second-round groups of 3 → SF / 3rd / Final. The format wasn't repeated; 1986 introduced the modern knockout from R16.

The 'Disgrace of Gijón' — West Germany 1-0 Austria, 25 June 1982. After Hrubesch's 10th-minute goal, both teams played out the rest of the match, knowing the result would advance both at Algeria's expense (Algeria had played the day before). FIFA changed the format the next tournament so final group games kicked off simultaneously.

Paolo Rossi won the Golden Boot (6 goals) and Golden Ball at 1982 — extraordinary because he had returned from a 2-year ban for match-fixing just months before the tournament. He scored a hat-trick vs Brazil (3-2), 2 in SF vs Poland, 1 in the final.

West Germany 5-4 France (3-3 after extra time) in the 1982 SF was the first penalty shootout in World Cup history. The match also featured Harald Schumacher's notorious challenge on Patrick Battiston — one of the most controversial fouls in WC history — for which no card was shown.
Algeria, Cameroon, Honduras, Kuwait, and New Zealand all made their World Cup debuts at 1982 — five debutants in one tournament, made possible by expansion to 24 teams.

Argentina 2-1 England in the 1986 QF on 22 June. Maradona scored both goals: the 'Hand of God' (illegal handball, 51') and four minutes later the 'Goal of the Century' (60-yard solo run past five Englishmen). Both moments came in the same match.

1986 introduced the modern KO bracket format: 6 groups of 4 → top 2 + 4 best 3rd-place teams advance to R16 → QF/SF/Final. The 1990 and 1994 editions used the same template before expansion to 32 teams in 1998.

Mexico became the first nation to host two World Cups (1970 + 1986). Mexico took over from original 1986 host Colombia, who withdrew citing economic crisis. Mexico, Italy (1934/1990), Germany (1974/2006), Brazil (1950/2014), and France (1938/1998) are the only repeat hosts.

Cameroon, led by 38-year-old Roger Milla, became the first African team to reach a World Cup quarter-final. They opened by upsetting reigning champions Argentina 1-0, then beat Romania 2-1 (Milla brace) and Colombia 2-1 a.e.t. (Milla brace) before losing 3-2 a.e.t. to England.

1990 had the lowest goals/match average since 1934 — 2.21. The goalfest 1954 (5.38) and modern WCs around 2.5-2.7 bracket the average. The defensive 1990 tournament prompted FIFA to award 3 points for a win in 1994 (instead of 2) to incentivise attacking play.

Salvatore 'Toto' Schillaci had 1 international cap and 0 goals before the 1990 WC. He came off the bench against Austria, scored, and ended the tournament with 6 goals — winning both the Golden Boot and Player of the Tournament for hosts Italy.

Brazil 0-0 Italy (3-2 pens) on 17 July 1994 in the Rose Bowl, Pasadena was the first World Cup final ever decided on penalties. Roberto Baggio missed the decisive kick — sending it over the bar. Brazil won their fourth title.

Russia's Oleg Salenko scored 5 goals in a single match — Russia 6-1 Cameroon, 28 June 1994 — the only player ever to do so at a World Cup. Russia were eliminated in the group stage; Salenko shared the Golden Boot with Hristo Stoichkov on 6 goals.
USA 1994 set the all-time WC attendance record: 3,587,538 fans (averaging 68,991/match) — a record that still stands more than 30 years later, even with WC expansions.
1994 was the first WC to award the Lev Yashin Trophy for Best Goalkeeper. Belgium's Michel Preud'homme was the inaugural winner. The trophy is named after the legendary Soviet keeper.

Colombian defender Andrés Escobar was murdered in Medellín on 2 July 1994, ten days after his own goal in Colombia's 2-1 loss to USA. The killing was widely linked to Colombian drug-trafficking betting interests; the gunman served 11 years.

1998 was the first WC with 32 teams (expanded from 24). Format: 8 groups of 4 → straight R16 → QF/SF/Final, with no 'best 3rd-place' wildcards. This format remained from 1998 through 2022 — the longest-running structure in WC history.
Zinedine Zidane scored two headers (27', 45') in France's 3-0 win over Brazil in the 1998 final at Stade de France. Petit added a third in the 93rd. France's first World Cup title.

Ronaldo collapsed with a seizure hours before the 1998 final and was initially left out of the team sheet. He was reinstated 45 minutes before kickoff under unclear circumstances. Brazil lost 0-3; Ronaldo played but was a shadow of himself. The cause has never been fully explained.

Croatia, on their first World Cup appearance as an independent nation, finished third — eliminating Germany 3-0 in the QF. Davor Šuker won the Golden Boot with 6 goals. They reached another final in 2018 (lost to France).

Iran 2-1 USA on 21 June 1998 was the first meeting between the two nations in any sport since the 1979 hostage crisis. The political weight made it one of the most-watched matches of the tournament. Iranian players gave the USA team white roses pre-match.

2002 was the first World Cup hosted in Asia and the first ever co-hosted (South Korea + Japan). Brazil's 5th title — Ronaldo scored 8 goals (incl. 2 in final vs Germany) for redemption after his 1998 final mystery seizure.
Oliver Kahn (Germany) became the only goalkeeper ever to win the Golden Ball (Best Player) at a World Cup, despite his error gifting Ronaldo the opening goal in the final.

Defending champions France crashed out in the group stage with 0 goals scored and 1 point — opening with a 1-0 loss to debutants Senegal. Zidane was injured and only played one match. Senegal then reached the QF on debut.

Zinedine Zidane's last act in football was headbutting Italy's Marco Materazzi in the chest in the 110th minute of the 2006 final. He was sent off; Italy won 5-3 on penalties after 1-1 a.e.t. Zidane still won Best Player.
2006 was the first WC with a Best Young Player award. The inaugural winner: Lukas Podolski (Germany), aged 21.

2010 was the first World Cup hosted in Africa. South Africa won the right amid heavy controversy (Sepp Blatter favoured the rotation). Spain became the first European nation ever to win a World Cup outside Europe.

Andrés Iniesta scored Spain's only goal of the 2010 final in the 116th minute — Spain 1-0 Netherlands a.e.t., in the dirtiest WC final ever (14 yellow cards + Heitinga red). It was Spain's first World Cup title; they became the first team to win a WC after losing their opening match.

The Mineirazo: Germany 7-1 Brazil in the SF on 8 July 2014 in Belo Horizonte. Germany scored 5 goals in 18 minutes (23'-29'). The worst defeat any host nation has ever suffered at a World Cup. Klose's 16th career WC goal in this match made him the all-time leading scorer (passing Ronaldo).

Luis Suárez bit Italy's Giorgio Chiellini on the shoulder in Uruguay's 1-0 win on 24 June 2014 — his third biting incident in football. FIFA banned him for 9 international matches and 4 months from all football. Uruguay still advanced.
Kylian Mbappé scored in France's 4-2 win over Croatia in the 2018 final at age 19 — the youngest scorer in a World Cup final since Pelé in 1958. He won Best Young Player.

2018 was the first World Cup with VAR (Video Assistant Referee). 2018 also set the record for own goals in a single tournament — 12 — partly because VAR's involvement created more deflected/handball decisions.

Defending champions Germany crashed out in the group stage at 2018 — losing to Mexico, beating Sweden, then losing 0-2 to South Korea in their final group match. The third reigning champion in four WCs to exit in groups (after France 2002 and Italy 2010).
2022 was the first World Cup ever played in winter (November-December), shifted from the traditional June-July window because of Qatar's summer heat. It was also the first WC in the Arab world.

Lionel Messi finally won the World Cup at his 5th attempt (age 35), scoring 7 goals + 3 assists. Argentina's 3rd title (1978, 1986, 2022). He won Best Player for the second time (also 2014). The final — Argentina 3-3 France (4-2 pens) — is widely considered the greatest WC final ever played.

Mbappé scored a hat-trick in the 2022 final — the first in a WC final since Geoff Hurst in 1966. He won the Golden Boot with 8 goals, but France lost 4-2 on penalties. He became the first player to win the Golden Boot at age 23 or younger and lose the final in the same tournament.

Saudi Arabia 2-1 Argentina, 22 November 2022 — one of the great World Cup upsets. The eventual champions Argentina lost their opener; only Spain (1950) and Spain (2010) had previously gone on to win the title after losing match one. Saudi declared a national holiday.

Morocco became the first African team — and first Arab nation — ever to reach a World Cup semi-final. Their run included beating Belgium, Spain (on pens), and Portugal before losing 2-0 to France in the SF.
2022 was the first WC with 26-player squads (up from 23, which had held since 2002). The change accommodated the winter timing and the more compressed 28-day tournament window.

2026 will be the first World Cup with 48 teams (up from 32). Format: 12 groups of 4 → top 2 + 8 best 3rd-place teams advance to a Round of 32 → R16 → QF → SF → 3rd → Final. Total of 104 matches (up from 64 in 2022).

2026 will be the first World Cup ever co-hosted by three nations: USA, Canada, and Mexico. Mexico will become the first nation to host three World Cups (1970, 1986, 2026). The final will be at MetLife Stadium in East Rutherford, NJ.
